one_line_ask: Take a client's 38-frame food shoot (flat-lay / 45° / side of one dish) that is close to the mark and grade it to a cohesive bright-and-airy look — white balance, exposure, highlight/shadow, gentle color — then deliver straightened, correctly-sized final files.
brief:
A food-photography client (single hero dish, shot three ways: an overhead flat-lay, a 45-degree three-quarter, and a side/profile shot, 38 frames total) hands you a set that is "close to the goal but needs tweaking." The goal is a consistent BRIGHT AND AIRY look across the whole set: clean neutral-to-slightly-warm white balance, lifted exposure, bright but not blown highlights on the plate and window light, open (not muddy) shadows, and gentle, appetising color — applied cohesively so the 38 frames read as one shoot. Quick turnaround / ASAP.

Concretely, working in the Adobe connector entirely on the client's own pixels (no compositing, no generative fill, no background swap):
1. Ingest the three representative hero frames the recipe is tuned on, plus license one neutral linen/marble backdrop reference from Adobe Stock for the social teaser at the end.
2. On the flat-lay, square it up (auto-straighten the overhead so the plate edges are true) and crop to a clean composition.
3. Build the bright-and-airy base grade on that straightened flat-lay: warm the white balance toward a clean daylight, lift exposure, pull the brightest highlights back so the window light and the plate rim are
